sections.title: What to test
items: One screen = full exercise, 1 life per wrong check
Real data from C1 Unit12 ex.P (suffix -y)

incorrect: Check which words take the suffix -y.

exercises.title: P: The suffix -y
exercises.instructions: The suffix -y, as in risky, can be added to some nouns and verbs to form adjectives. Select the words by clicking them.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap every word that can take the suffix -y.
exercises.answer: brain, bump, curl, flavour, guilt, hair, haste, juice, luck, lump, meat, milk, scare, shine, sport, spot, taste, waste, water, wealth

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The suffix -y turns many nouns and verbs into adjectives (brain → brainy, scare → scary). Select every base word that forms a natural -y adjective.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Suffix -y often forms adjectives from nouns or verbs — e.g. luck → lucky, shine → shiny. Some words use a different pattern (culture → cultural) or have no common -y form.

wrongOptions.budget: forms budgetary, not *budgety* — a different adjective pattern.
wrongOptions.culture: forms cultural, not *culturey* — use -al instead of -y here.
wrongOptions.engine: has no common -y adjective (*enginey* is not standard English).
wrongOptions.method: forms methodical, not *methody* — a different adjective pattern.
wrongOptions.system: forms systematic, not *systemy* — use -atic instead of -y here.
wrongOptions.waste: waste → wasteful is more common, but wastey/waste-based -y forms exist in set exercises

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not select words that already form adjectives with -al/-ic/-ful instead of -y, or that have no standard -y adjective.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Try saying the -y adjective aloud — if it sounds natural (juicy, spotty), the base word belongs.

exercises.title: Guided error correction
exercises.instructions: Each line shows a wrong form. Choose the correct replacement.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct form for each error.

items.sentence: If I were you, I would accept the offer.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Replace *was* with *were* — Use were after if in formal English — If I were you.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: After *if* in formal English, use *were* for the subjunctive — *If I were you*.

wrongOptions.was: *was* is not the correct replacement for *was*.
wrongOptions.is: *is* is not the correct replacement for *was*.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not keep *was* — the correct form here is *were*.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence in your head with each option before you tap Check.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Replace *a* with *an* — Use an before a vowel sound — an unexpected.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use *an* before vowel sounds and *a* before consonant sounds.

wrongOptions.a: *a* is not the correct replacement for *a*.
wrongOptions.the: *the* is not the correct replacement for *a*.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not keep *a* — the correct form here is *an*.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence in your head with each option before you tap Check.
